items.title: Identifying S. aureus: Key Diagnostic Features
items.content: S. aureus can be distinguished from other staphylococci by its unique characteristics, which are crucial for accurate diagnosis and management.
keyPoints: S. aureus is the most virulent among ~40 staphylococcal species.
Produces coagulase, converting fibrinogen to fibrin.
Ferments mannitol and produces DNAse.
Forms golden $\beta$-hemolytic colonies on blood agar plates.
Positive for protein A; latex kits can detect both protein A and clumping factor for rapid identification.
items.reference: Staphylococcal Infections · Staphylococcal Infections | MICROBIOLOGY AND TAXONOMY

items.title: Outbreak Investigation: Molecular Typing of Staphylococci
items.content: Molecular typing is essential for investigating nosocomial outbreaks to determine if multiple staphylococcal isolates are from a common source.
keyPoints: Concerns about nosocomial outbreaks may require molecular typing methods.
Pulsed-field gel electrophoresis and sequence-based techniques like SpA typing have been used historically.
Whole-genome sequencing is now the gold standard for discriminating among different staphylococcal isolates.
items.reference: Staphylococcal Infections · Staphylococcal Infections | MICROBIOLOGY AND TAXONOMY

items.title: Colonization and Infection Dynamics of S. aureus
items.content: S. aureus is both a commensal organism and an opportunistic pathogen, with colonization rates in healthy individuals ranging from 20-40%. Persistent colonization by the same strain occurs in about 10% of these cases. Colonization sites include the anterior nares, oropharynx, skin (especially when damaged), axilla, vagina, and perineum.
keyPoints: Colonization rates: 20–40% in healthy individuals
Persistent colonization by same strain: ~10%
Common colonization sites: anterior nares, oropharynx, skin, axilla, vagina, perineum

items.title: Risk Factors and Transmission of S. aureus Infections
items.content: S. aureus infections often arise from strains already present in an individual's commensal flora. Risk factors include diabetes, immunodeficiencies (e.g., neutropenia), skin abnormalities, and the presence of prosthetic devices. Person-to-person transmission is common through direct contact or aerosols.
keyPoints: Infections typically arise from existing commensal strains
Risk factors: Diabetes, immunodeficiencies, skin abnormalities, prosthetics
Transmission: Direct contact, aerosolized secretions

items.title: Epidemiological Trends of MRSA Infections
items.content: There has been a significant shift in the epidemiology of MRSA over recent decades. MRSA is now both a nosocomial and community-associated pathogen, with outbreaks occurring among diverse groups such as children, athletes, and drug users. The strain sequence type 8 (USA300) is predominant in the U.S., with 5-10% of infections being invasive.
keyPoints: MRSA: Nosocomial and community-associated
Outbreaks in diverse populations: Children, athletes, drug users
Predominant strain in U.S.: Sequence type 8 (USA300)
Invasive infection rate: 5–10%

items.title: Mechanisms of S. aureus Infection Progression
items.content: S. aureus induces abscess formation through an inflammatory response involving PMNs, macrophages, and fibroblasts. This can lead to either containment by a fibrinous capsule or spread to adjacent tissues or bloodstream.
keyPoints: Initial intense infiltration of polymorphonuclear leukocytes (PMNs)
Subsequent infiltration of macrophages and fibroblasts
Formation of a fibrinous capsule containing the infection

items.title: Toxin-Mediated Staphylococcal Diseases
items.content: In diseases like staphylococcal food poisoning and toxic shock syndrome, symptoms can occur without active infection due to the presence of enterotoxins or conditions promoting toxin elaboration.
keyPoints: Staphylococcal food poisoning occurs with heat-stable enterotoxin release
Toxic shock syndrome initiated by conditions like superabsorbent tampons

items.title: Regulation of S. aureus Virulence Gene Expression
items.content: The expression of virulence genes in S. aureus is regulated by genes such as agr and sar, which control the sequential release of bacterial elements necessary for successful invasion.
keyPoints: agr gene involved in quorum-sensing signal transduction
Sequential expression of adhesins and enzymes required for colonization and spread

items.title: Role of MSCRAMMs in S. aureus Adherence and Pathogenesis
items.content: MSCRAMMs, such as clumping factor and collagen-binding protein, enable S. aureus to adhere to host tissue surfaces by binding to matrix molecules like fibrinogen and collagen. This adherence is crucial for the pathogenesis of invasive infections including endocarditis and septic arthritis.
keyPoints: MSCRAMMs facilitate bacterial colonization on host tissues.
Critical in the development of invasive infections such as endocarditis and septic arthritis.

items.title: S. aureus Evasion of Host Immune Mechanisms
items.content: S. aureus employs multiple strategies to evade the host immune system, including antiphagocytic polysaccharide microcapsules and protein A that binds IgG Fc portions, preventing opsonophagocytosis. Additionally, CHIPS and EAP interfere with PMN migration.
keyPoints: Antiphagocytic capsule types 5 and 8 are common in human S. aureus infections.
Protein A prevents opsonophagocytosis by binding IgG Fc portions.
CHIPS and EAP inhibit PMN migration to infection sites.

items.title: Virulence Determinants in CA-MRSA Infections
items.content: CA-MRSA infections are associated with specific virulence determinants such as the Panton-Valentine leukocidin gene, ACME, and phenol-soluble modulins. These factors contribute to the pathogenicity of SSTIs and necrotizing pneumonia.
keyPoints: Panton-Valentine leukocidin linked with SSTIs and necrotizing pneumonia.
ACME may aid in evasion of host defenses.
Phenol-soluble modulins are cytolytic peptides enhancing virulence.

items.title: Mechanism of TSS and Food Poisoning by TSST-1
items.content: TSST-1 and enterotoxins act as superantigens, binding directly to the invariant region of MHC class II outside the groove. This leads to massive T-cell activation (up to 20% of total T-cells), causing a cytokine storm with inflammatory mediators such as interferon γ, IL-1, IL-6, TNF-α, and TNF-β.
keyPoints: TSST-1 and enterotoxins function as T-cell mitogens.
Binding occurs outside the MHC class II groove.
Up to 20% of total T-cells can be activated.
Cytokine storm includes interferon γ, IL-1, IL-6, TNF-α, and TNF-β.

items.title: Pathogenesis of SSSS by Exfoliative Toxins
items.content: Exfoliative toxins ETA and ETB are serine proteases that cleave desmosomal cadherins in the skin's superficial layer, causing epidermal splitting at the granular level. This results in the characteristic superficial desquamation seen in SSSS.
keyPoints: ETA and ETB are responsible for SSSS.
Toxins act as serine proteases.
Cleavage of desmosomal cadherins occurs.
Epidermal splitting at the granular level leads to superficial desquamation.

items.title: Diagnosing Staphylococcal Infections: Importance of Gram's Stain and PCR
items.content: Staphylococcal infections can be diagnosed effectively using Gram's stain and microscopic examination of abscess contents or infected tissue. Additionally, PCR-based assays are increasingly utilized for rapid identification of S. aureus infection.
keyPoints: Gram's stain and microscopic examination provide immediate diagnostic information.
Routine cultures of infected material usually yield positive results.
PCR-based assays offer a rapid diagnosis method for S. aureus infections.

items.title: Challenges in Diagnosing Endovascular Infections with Staphylococcus aureus Bacteremia
items.content: Determining the presence of infective endocarditis or a metastatic focus in patients with S. aureus bacteremia is challenging. Uniformly positive blood cultures over time suggest an endovascular infection such as endocarditis.
keyPoints: S. aureus bacteremia can indicate more severe infections like endocarditis.
Uniformly positive blood cultures collected over time are indicative of potential endovascular infections.

items.title: Recognizing and Managing S. aureus Skin Infections
items.content: S. aureus is a common cause of various skin infections, including folliculitis, furuncles, carbuncles, impetigo, and cellulitis. These conditions often arise in individuals with predisposing factors such as chronic skin conditions, skin damage, injections, or poor hygiene.
keyPoints: Folliculitis involves the hair follicle with central purulence surrounded by induration and erythema.
Furuncles are painful abscesses extending from the hair follicle, often in hairy, moist areas.
Carbuncles result from coalescence of lesions into deeper subcutaneous tissue, typically on the lower neck.
S. aureus is a leading cause of surgical wound infections.

items.title: Diagnosing and Treating S. aureus Musculoskeletal Infections
items.content: S. aureus can cause osteomyelitis, which may present with fever, bone pain, or reluctance to bear weight in children, and back pain with possible neurologic symptoms in adults. MRI is the most reliable imaging modality for diagnosis.
keyPoints: Hematogenous osteomyelitis in children often involves long bones; blood cultures positive in ~50% of cases.
Vertebral osteomyelitis common in patients with endocarditis, hemodialysis, diabetics, and injection drug users.
MRI is the most reliable imaging modality for diagnosing osteomyelitis.
S. aureus is the leading cause of septic arthritis, presenting with intense joint pain, swelling, fever, and turbid fluid (>50,000 PMNs/μL) on aspiration.

items.title: Understanding Mastitis in Nursing Mothers
items.content: Mastitis is an infection of the breast that occurs in 1–3% of nursing mothers, typically presenting with symptoms ranging from cellulitis to abscess formation within 2–3 weeks post-delivery.
keyPoints: Occurs in 1–3% of nursing mothers.
Presents within 2–3 weeks after delivery.
Symptoms range from cellulitis to abscess formation, with systemic signs like fever and chills in severe cases.

items.title: S. aureus Respiratory Infections: Recognizing Complications
items.content: Staphylococcus aureus can cause serious respiratory infections, particularly in newborns and infants, presenting with symptoms like shortness of breath, fever, and respiratory failure. Chest x-rays may show pneumatoceles, while complications include pneumothorax and empyema.
keyPoints: S. aureus is a significant cause of serious respiratory infections in newborns and infants.
Chest x-ray findings can reveal pneumatoceles (shaggy, thin-walled cavities).
Recognized complications include pneumothorax and empyema.

items.title: S. aureus Bacteremia: Understanding Complications and Risk Factors
items.content: S. aureus bacteremia can lead to severe complications such as sepsis, endocarditis, vasculitis, or metastatic seeding with an estimated frequency of up to 31%. Comorbid conditions like diabetes, HIV infection, and renal insufficiency increase complication risks.
keyPoints: Complications from S. aureus bacteremia can include sepsis, endocarditis, vasculitis, or metastatic seeding (up to 31% frequency).
Risk factors for complications include diabetes, HIV infection, and renal insufficiency.
Community-acquired bacteremia, lack of primary infection focus, and prosthetic devices increase complication risk.

items.title: S. aureus Endocarditis: Clinical Presentation and Risk Factors
items.content: The incidence of S. aureus endocarditis has increased, now accounting for 25–35% of cases globally. It is associated with intravascular devices and injection drug use, with a mortality rate ranging from 20 to 40%. Key risk factors include hemodialysis, prosthetic devices, and immunosuppression.
keyPoints: S. aureus endocarditis accounts for 25–35% of cases worldwide.
Increased incidence linked to intravascular device use and injection drug use.
Mortality rates range from 20 to 40%, with risk factors including hemodialysis, prosthetic devices, and immunosuppression.

items.title: Left-Sided Native-Valve Endocarditis: Increased Risk and Complications
items.content: Patients with pre-existing cardiac valvular damage are more likely to develop left-sided native-valve endocarditis, which presents a worse prognosis due to increased complications.
keyPoints: More common in older patients compared to right-sided endocarditis.
Increased incidence of complications: peripheral emboli, cardiac decompensation, cerebrovascular events, and metastatic seeding.

items.title: S. aureus Prosthetic-Valve Endocarditis: Urgent Intervention Required
items.content: Prosthetic-valve endocarditis caused by S. aureus is particularly severe in the early postoperative period, often necessitating urgent valve replacement due to high morbidity and mortality.
keyPoints: Fulminant infection especially soon after surgery.
Medical therapy alone is usually insufficient; urgent valve replacement may be necessary.
Patients prone to valvular insufficiency or myocardial abscesses.

items.title: Community-Acquired MRSA: Recognizing Life-Threatening Infections
items.content: CA-MRSA infections, though often involving skin and soft tissues, can lead to severe invasive conditions such as necrotizing fasciitis and sepsis.
keyPoints: 5–10% of CA-MRSA infections are invasive and life-threatening.
Increased virulence of CA-MRSA strains contributes to severe manifestations like necrotizing pneumonia and Waterhouse-Friderichsen syndrome.

items.title: Recognizing Toxic Shock Syndrome (TSS): Key Diagnostic Criteria and Red Flags
items.content: Toxic Shock Syndrome (TSS), caused by Staphylococcus aureus enterotoxins, presents with specific clinical criteria. Recognizing these signs is crucial for early diagnosis and management to prevent rapid progression.
keyPoints: Fever: temperature ≥ 102.0°F (≥ 38.9°C)
Rash: diffuse macular erythroderma
Desquamation occurs 1–2 weeks after rash onset
Hypotension: systolic blood pressure ≤ 90 mmHg for adults or less than the fifth percentile, by age, for children <16 years old

Multisystem involvement (≥3 organ systems): gastrointestinal (vomiting or diarrhea at illness onset), muscular (severe myalgia or creatine phosphokinase level at least twice ULN), mucous membrane (hyperemia), renal (blood urea nitrogen or creatinine level at least twice ULN, pyuria without UTI), hepatic (total bilirubin or aminotransferase level at least twice ULN), CNS (disorientation without focal signs in absence of fever and hypotension), hematologic (platelet count <10^5/mL)
items.reference: Staphylococcal Infections · CLINICAL SYNDROMES (Table 152-1) (part 4) | Clinical Criteria

items.title: Differentiating Menstrual vs. Nonmenstrual TSS: Enterotoxin Involvement
items.content: Understanding the differences between menstrual and nonmenstrual TSS is important for identifying the causative toxins, which guides management strategies.
keyPoints: More than 90% of menstrual cases are caused by TSST-1
A high percentage of nonmenstrual cases are caused by enterotoxins (e.g., enterotoxin B)
Menstrual TSS onset usually occurs 2 or 3 days after the start of menstruation
items.reference: Staphylococcal Infections · CLINICAL SYNDROMES (Table 152-1) (part 4) | Clinical Criteria

items.subtopic: Laboratory Criteria | Case Classification | NON-S. AUREUS STAPHYLOCOCCAL INFECTIONS
items.title: Diagnosis of Staphylococcal Infections: Laboratory and Clinical Criteria
items.content: The diagnosis of staphylococcal infections, including non-S. aureus types, relies on a combination of laboratory criteria and clinical findings. Laboratory tests should show negative results for other pathogens in blood or cerebrospinal fluid cultures and serologic tests for Rocky Mountain spotted fever, leptospirosis, or measles. Case classification is probable if four out of five clinical criteria are met, and confirmed if all five are met, including desquamation unless the patient dies before this occurs.
keyPoints: Negative blood or cerebrospinal fluid cultures for other pathogens
Serologic tests negative for Rocky Mountain spotted fever, leptospirosis, or measles
Probable case: meets laboratory criteria and four of five clinical criteria
Confirmed case: meets laboratory criteria and all five clinical criteria including desquamation
items.reference: Staphylococcal Infections · Laboratory Criteria | Case Classification | NON-S. AUREUS STAPHYLOCOCCAL INFECTIONS

items.subtopic: Laboratory Criteria | Case Classification | NON-S. AUREUS STAPHYLOCOCCAL INFECTIONS
items.title: Non-S. aureus Staphylococcal Infections: Clinical Impact and Common Pathogens
items.content: Non-S. aureus staphylococcal (NSaS) infections, though less virulent than S. aureus, are significant causes of prosthetic-device infections, including endocarditis, and can lead to severe bloodstream infections in neonates and neutropenic patients. Staphylococcus epidermidis is the most common NSaS pathogen, part of normal human flora found on skin, oropharynx, and vagina.
keyPoints: NSaS are major causes of prosthetic-device infections
Staphylococcus epidermidis: most common NSaS pathogen
Part of normal human flora; abundant on skin
items.reference: Staphylococcal Infections · Laboratory Criteria | Case Classification | NON-S. AUREUS STAPHYLOCOCCAL INFECTIONS

items.title: Understanding S. epidermidis Biofilm Formation on Prosthetic Devices
items.content: S. epidermidis is particularly adept at causing prosthetic-device infections due to its ability to form biofilms, which protect the bacteria from host defenses and antibiotics.
keyPoints: Biofilm formation involves initial adhesion facilitated by surface-associated proteins like autolysin (AtlE), fibrinogen-binding protein, and accumulation-associated protein (AAP).
Extracellular polysaccharide (glycocalyx or slime) is crucial for biofilm development.
Intercellular adhesin (ica) genes are more prevalent in device-associated S. epidermidis strains than those colonizing mucosal surfaces.

items.title: Interpreting NSaS Blood Culture Results for True Bacteremia
items.content: While detecting NSaS in cultures is straightforward, distinguishing true bacteremia from contamination requires careful clinical correlation.
keyPoints: Only 10–20% of blood cultures positive for NSaS indicate true bacteremia due to skin contamination.
Clinical signs suggestive of true bacteremia include fever, local infection evidence (e.g., erythema or purulent drainage), leukocytosis, and systemic sepsis signs.
Laboratory indicators of true bacteremia are repeated isolation of the same strain from separate cultures, growth within 48 hours, and bacterial presence in both aerobic and anaerobic bottles.

items.subtopic: CLINICAL SYNDROMES | GENERAL PRINCIPLES OF THERAPY | DURATION OF ANTIMICROBIAL THERAPY
items.title: Recognizing and Managing Prosthetic Device-Related Staphylococcal Infections
items.content: Prosthetic device-related infections caused by NSaS often present with subtle localized signs, slow progression, and limited systemic findings. Fever may be present but is not always observed, and mild leukocytosis can occur. Acute-phase reactants such as erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein levels are typically elevated.
keyPoints: Subtle clinical presentation with possible purulent drainage or pain at the site
Fever is frequently but not always present
Mild leukocytosis may occur
Elevated acute-phase reactants: erythrocyte sedimentation rate, C-reactive protein
items.reference: Staphylococcal Infections · CLINICAL SYNDROMES | GENERAL PRINCIPLES OF THERAPY | DURATION OF ANTIMICROBIAL THERAPY

items.subtopic: CLINICAL SYNDROMES | GENERAL PRINCIPLES OF THERAPY | DURATION OF ANTIMICROBIAL THERAPY
items.title: Determining Duration of Antimicrobial Therapy for S. aureus Bacteremia
items.content: Therapy duration for S. aureus bacteremia is typically 4–6 weeks due to high complication risks, such as endocarditis and metastatic infections. Uncomplicated bacteremias may be treated with a short course (2 weeks) if specific criteria are met: removable focus of infection, prompt response to therapy, no evidence of metastatic foci, and absence of implanted prostheses.
keyPoints: Prolonged therapy for S. aureus bacteremia is 4–6 weeks
Criteria for short-course therapy (2 weeks): removable focus, prompt response, no metastatic foci, no implanted prostheses
Transesophageal echocardiography is necessary to rule out endocarditis
items.reference: Staphylococcal Infections · CLINICAL SYNDROMES | GENERAL PRINCIPLES OF THERAPY | DURATION OF ANTIMICROBIAL THERAPY

items.title: Understanding Methicillin Resistance in Staphylococcal Infections
items.content: Methicillin resistance in staphylococci, particularly MRSA, is a critical concern due to the prevalence of multidrug-resistant strains. The mecA gene, part of the SCCmec element, leads to methicillin resistance by producing PBP2a, which alters susceptibility profiles.
keyPoints: Methicillin resistance indicates resistance to all SPRPs and most cephalosporins except ceftaroline.
>80% of nosocomial staphylococcal isolates are resistant to methicillin.
40–50% of S. aureus isolates in many U.S. hospitals are MRSA.
Methicillin resistance is due to the production of PBP2a, encoded by the mecA gene.
items.reference: Staphylococcal Infections · CHOICE OF ANTIMICROBIAL AGENTS (part 1)

items.title: Treatment Options for Methicillin-Resistant Staphylococcal Infections
items.content: For invasive MRSA infections, vancomycin or daptomycin is recommended. However, vancomycin's efficacy against methicillin-susceptible strains surpasses that of SPRPs, and its effectiveness against MRSA has decreased in some regions.
keyPoints: Vancomycin or daptomycin are preferred for invasive MRSA infections.
Vancomycin is less effective than SPRPs for methicillin-susceptible S. aureus.
MRSA susceptibility to vancomycin has decreased globally.
items.reference: Staphylococcal Infections · CHOICE OF ANTIMICROBIAL AGENTS (part 1)

items.title: Understanding Staphylococcal Resistance Mechanisms
items.content: Staphylococcus aureus has developed resistance to vancomycin through three mechanisms: MIC creep, VISA, and VRSA. MIC creep involves an incremental increase in vancomycin MICs, with strains showing MICs ≥1.5 µg/mL linked to higher morbidity and mortality. VISA evolves under vancomycin pressure, characterized by a thickened cell wall trapping the drug. VRSA acquires resistance via horizontal gene transfer from Enterococcus faecalis, involving the vanA gene.
keyPoints: MIC creep: S. aureus strains with MICs ≥1.5 µg/mL are associated with increased morbidity and mortality.
VISA mechanism: Thickened cell wall traps vancomycin; involves regulatory genes in cell wall metabolism.
VRSA resistance: Acquired via horizontal transfer from Enterococcus faecalis, involving the vanA gene.
items.reference: Staphylococcal Infections · CHOICE OF ANTIMICROBIAL AGENTS (part 2)

items.title: Daptomycin Use and Monitoring
items.content: Daptomycin is effective for treating bacteremia and complicated skin infections but not respiratory infections. It disrupts the cytoplasmic membrane of bacteria. Resistance can develop during therapy, especially in patients previously treated with vancomycin. Monitoring for rhabdomyolysis (via creatine phosphokinase) and eosinophilic pneumonia is essential.
keyPoints: Daptomycin indications: Bacteremia, right-sided endocarditis, complicated skin infections.
Mechanism of action: Disruption of cytoplasmic membrane.
Monitoring: Rhabdomyolysis (creatinine phosphokinase) and eosinophilic pneumonia.
items.reference: Staphylococcal Infections · CHOICE OF ANTIMICROBIAL AGENTS (part 2)

items.title: Ceftaroline for MRSA Infections
items.content: Ceftaroline, a fifth-generation cephalosporin, is effective against MRSA, including strains with reduced susceptibility to vancomycin and daptomycin. It is approved for nosocomial pneumonias and skin and soft tissue infections (SSTIs) and can be used for invasive MRSA infections.
keyPoints: Ceftaroline activity: Bactericidal against MRSA, including strains with reduced susceptibility to vancomycin and daptomycin.
Indications: Nosocomial pneumonias, SSTIs, invasive MRSA infections.
items.reference: Staphylococcal Infections · CHOICE OF ANTIMICROBIAL AGENTS (part 2)

items.title: Tigecycline Use and Limitations
items.content: Tigecycline is a broad-spectrum minocycline analogue with bacteriostatic activity against MRSA, approved for SSTIs and intraabdominal infections caused by S. aureus. However, it is not recommended for invasive infections.
keyPoints: Bacteriostatic activity against MRSA
Approved for SSTIs and intraabdominal infections
Not recommended for invasive infections
items.reference: Staphylococcal Infections · CHOICE OF ANTIMICROBIAL AGENTS (part 3)

items.title: Ceftobiprole Efficacy in Staphylococcal Infections
items.content: Ceftobiprole is a new antibiotic with excellent activity against both MRSA and MSSA, effective in treating complicated staphylococcal bacteremias, SSTIs, and pneumonia.
keyPoints: Excellent activity against MRSA and MSSA
Effective for complicated staphylococcal bacteremias
Approved for use in SSTIs and pneumonia
items.reference: Staphylococcal Infections · CHOICE OF ANTIMICROBIAL AGENTS (part 3)

items.title: Empirical Therapy for MRSA: Vancomycin or Daptomycin
items.content: When treating suspected MRSA infections without known antibiotic susceptibility, empirical therapy with vancomycin or daptomycin is recommended. The choice between these agents may depend on local resistance patterns, particularly if elevated vancomycin MICs (>1.5 µg/mL) are prevalent.
keyPoints: Empirical coverage for MRSA indicated when susceptibility unknown.
Vancomycin or daptomycin generally recommended.
Uncertain preference for daptomycin with elevated vancomycin MICs (>1.5 µg/mL).
items.reference: Staphylococcal Infections · ANTIMICROBIAL THERAPY FOR SELECTED SETTINGS

items.title: Salvage Therapy for Persistent MRSA Bacteremia
items.content: For complicated S. aureus infections with persistent bacteremia (≥3 days), combination therapy may be necessary. Daptomycin or vancomycin combined with a β-lactam antibiotic, such as ceftaroline, enhances bactericidal activity by altering bacterial cell-surface charge and binding dynamics.
keyPoints: Salvage therapy needed for persistent MRSA bacteremia (≥3 days).
Combination of daptomycin/vancomycin with β-lactam (e.g., ceftaroline) enhances efficacy.
Other combinations include trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole or rifampin with daptomycin.
items.reference: Staphylococcal Infections · ANTIMICROBIAL THERAPY FOR SELECTED SETTINGS

items.title: Antimicrobial Therapy for S. aureus Endocarditis
items.content: S. aureus endocarditis requires prompt empirical therapy following blood culture collection. For native-valve endocarditis, β-lactam is recommended; if MRSA is isolated, vancomycin (15–20 mg/kg every 8–12 h) or daptomycin (6–10 mg/kg daily) is used. Prosthetic-valve endocarditis often necessitates surgery and a combination of antibiotics including β-lactams, gentamicin, and rifampin.
keyPoints: Prompt empirical therapy for S. aureus endocarditis after blood culture collection.
Native-valve: β-lactam; MRSA: vancomycin (15–20 mg/kg every 8–12 h) or daptomycin (6–10 mg/kg daily).
Prosthetic-valve: Surgery + antibiotics (β-lactam, gentamicin, rifampin for ≥6 weeks).
items.reference: Staphylococcal Infections · ANTIMICROBIAL THERAPY FOR SELECTED SETTINGS

items.subtopic: NONTRADITIONAL APPROACHES TO ANTISTAPHYLOCOCCAL THERAPY | ☐ PREVENTION
items.title: Decolonization Strategies to Reduce S. aureus Infections
items.content: Decolonization strategies using topical agents like mupirocin and chlorhexidine can effectively reduce the incidence of postsurgical infections in individuals colonized with S. aureus, particularly in high-risk settings such as intensive care units.
keyPoints: Decolonization reduces incidence of postsurgical infections among nasally colonized individuals.
Risk of recurrent admissions for S. aureus bacteremia is ~22% within 30 days post-discharge.
Mupirocin and chlorhexidine decolonization lowers recurrence rates of infections.
items.reference: Staphylococcal Infections · NONTRADITIONAL APPROACHES TO ANTISTAPHYLOCOCCAL THERAPY | ☐ PREVENTION

items.subtopic: NONTRADITIONAL APPROACHES TO ANTISTAPHYLOCOCCAL THERAPY | ☐ PREVENTION
items.title: Bundling to Prevent Nosocomial Infections
items.content: The use of 'bundling'—a sequence of prescribed medical interventions—has been effective in reducing nosocomial infections related to procedures like intravenous catheter insertion, where staphylococci are common pathogens.
keyPoints: 'Bundling' reduces rates of nosocomial infections from procedures such as IV catheter insertions.
Staphylococci are among the most common pathogens in these infections.
items.reference: Staphylococcal Infections · NONTRADITIONAL APPROACHES TO ANTISTAPHYLOCOCCAL THERAPY | ☐ PREVENTION

items.question: Which characteristic is used to generally distinguish S. aureus from other staphylococcal species?
items.explanation: S. aureus is generally distinguished from other staphylococcal species by coagulase production.
items.reference: Staphylococcal Infections · Staphylococcal Infections | MICROBIOLOGY AND TAXONOMY

items.question: What type of hemolysis do S. aureus colonies exhibit on blood agar plates?
items.explanation: S. aureus forms golden $\beta$-hemolytic colonies on blood agar plates.
items.reference: Staphylococcal Infections · Staphylococcal Infections | MICROBIOLOGY AND TAXONOMY

items.question: Which method is considered the gold standard for discriminating among different staphylococcal isolates?
items.explanation: Whole-genome sequencing has emerged as the gold standard for discrimination among different staphylococcal isolates.
items.reference: Staphylococcal Infections · Staphylococcal Infections | MICROBIOLOGY AND TAXONOMY

items.question: Which site is NOT frequently colonized by S. aureus?
items.explanation: The liver is not mentioned as a frequent colonization site for S. aureus.

items.question: Which group of individuals has an increased rate of S. aureus colonization and uses injectable insulin?
items.explanation: Type 1 diabetics have an increased rate of S. aureus colonization and use injectable insulin.

items.question: What is a common cause of surgical wound infections?
items.explanation: S. aureus is the most common cause of surgical wound infections.

items.question: What has been a major change in the epidemiology of MRSA over the past three decades?
options: Shift from hospital to community settings
items.explanation: There has been a shift of MRSA from being primarily a nosocomial pathogen to an established community-based pathogen.

items.question: Which of the following is a characteristic feature of S. aureus pathogenesis?
options: S. aureus induces abscess formation at local and distant sites.
S. aureus primarily causes infections without an inflammatory response.
Infection with S. aureus cannot spread to adjoining tissues or into the bloodstream.
Toxin-mediated diseases by S. aureus always require viable bacteria for symptom development.
items.explanation: S. aureus is known for its capacity to induce abscess formation at both local and distant sites, defining its pathogenic framework.

items.question: In staphylococcal food poisoning, what can lead to symptom development?
options: Presence of viable bacteria in the food.
Release of heat-stable enterotoxin into the food.
Exposure to low temperatures during food preparation.
items.explanation: Symptoms can develop due to the release of heat-stable enterotoxin, even in the absence of viable bacteria.

items.question: What is a common feature among different S. aureus strains as revealed by genome studies?
options: High degree of nucleotide sequence similarity in core genomes.
Complete absence of genetic material acquired through horizontal transfer.
Lack of mobile genetic elements containing toxin genes.
Absence of methicillin resistance gene mecA.
items.explanation: The complete genomes reveal a high degree of nucleotide sequence similarity in the core genomes of different strains.

items.question: Which regulatory gene is part of a quorum-sensing signal transduction pathway in S. aureus?
items.explanation: The agr gene is part of the quorum-sensing signal transduction pathway that responds to bacterial density.

items.question: Which S. aureus surface proteins play a crucial role in adherence to host tissue surfaces?
items.explanation: MSCRAMMs (microbial surface components recognizing adhesive matrix molecules) are crucial for adherence to host tissue surfaces.

items.question: What is the role of S. aureus lipases in infection?
options: Facilitate bacterial survival in lipid-rich areas
items.explanation: Lipases facilitate bacterial survival in lipid-rich areas such as hair follicles.

items.question: Which staphylococcal toxin is cytolytic to PMNs, macrophages, and monocytes?
items.explanation: Panton-Valentine leukocidin is cytolytic to PMNs, macrophages, and monocytes.

items.question: Which component of the S. aureus cell wall can initiate an inflammatory response leading to sepsis?
items.explanation: The S. aureus cell wall component, lipoteichoic acid, can initiate an inflammatory response leading to sepsis.

items.question: Which antibodies have been shown to facilitate opsonization in vitro and protect against infection in animal models?
items.explanation: Anticapsular and anti-MSCRAMM antibodies facilitate opsonization in vitro and have been protective in animal models.

items.question: What is the primary mechanism by which TSST-1 causes toxic shock syndrome?
options: Directly binds to MHC class II groove
Binds directly to T-cell receptors via the $ \nu\beta $ chain, causing a cytokine storm
Cleaves desmosomal cadherins in the skin
Stimulates the vagus nerve and vomiting center
items.explanation: TSST-1 binds directly to T-cell receptors via the $ \nu\beta $ chain, causing a cytokine storm.

items.question: What is responsible for the symptoms of staphylococcal food poisoning?
options: A different region of the enterotoxin molecule
items.explanation: A different region of the enterotoxin molecule is responsible for symptoms of food poisoning.

items.question: Which method is commonly used for the rapid diagnosis of S. aureus infection?
items.explanation: PCR-based assays are now often used for the rapid diagnosis of S. aureus infection.

items.question: What is a diagnostic challenge when dealing with patients who have documented S. aureus bacteremia?
options: Determining if they are colonized with MRSA
Identifying the presence of infective endocarditis or metastatic infection
Confirming positive cultures from blood samples
Using Gram's stain for initial diagnosis
items.explanation: Determining whether patients with documented S. aureus bacteremia also have infective endocarditis or a metastatic focus of infection remains a diagnostic challenge.

items.question: What does uniformly positive cultures of blood collected over time suggest?
items.explanation: Uniformly positive cultures of blood collected over time suggest an endovascular infection such as endocarditis.

items.question: Which condition commonly presents in nursing mothers and can range from cellulitis to abscess formation?
items.explanation: Mastitis develops in nursing mothers, presenting with symptoms ranging from cellulitis to abscess formation.

items.question: What is the most common cause of surgical wound infections?
items.explanation: S. aureus is one of the most common causes of surgical wound infections.

items.question: Which imaging modality is considered most reliable for diagnosing osteomyelitis?
items.explanation: Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) is the most reliable imaging modality to help establish the diagnosis of osteomyelitis.

items.question: Which type of S. aureus infection involves hair follicles and can spread to adjoining tissues?
items.explanation: S. aureus cutaneous infections such as folliculitis involve the hair follicle and can spread to adjoining tissues.

items.question: Which complication is commonly associated with S. aureus bacteremia?
items.explanation: S. aureus bacteremia may be complicated by sepsis, endocarditis, vasculitis, or metastatic seeding.

items.question: What is a common clinical presentation of community-acquired S. aureus respiratory tract infections?
items.explanation: Community-acquired respiratory tract infections due to S. aureus often present with fever, bloody sputum production.

items.question: Which diagnostic method is less sensitive but less invasive for identifying valvular vegetations in S. aureus endocarditis?
items.explanation: Transthoracic echocardiography is less sensitive than transesophageal echocardiography but is less invasive.

items.question: Which factor increases the risk of S. aureus endocarditis?
items.explanation: The presence of intravascular prosthetic devices at the time of bacteremia increases the risk of endocarditis.

items.question: Which type of endocarditis is more commonly associated with S. aureus in the early postoperative period?
items.explanation: S. aureus is a common cause of prosthetic-valve endocarditis, especially in the early postoperative period.

items.question: What is often necessary for treating S. aureus prosthetic-valve endocarditis?
items.explanation: In most instances, medical therapy alone is not sufficient for S. aureus prosthetic-valve endocarditis; urgent valve replacement is necessary.

items.question: What percentage of nosocomial endocarditis cases are caused by S. aureus?
items.explanation: Nosocomial endocarditis is most commonly caused by S. aureus, accounting for 15–30% of cases.

items.question: Which type of infection often requires the removal of the device for successful therapy?
items.explanation: Successful therapy for S. aureus prosthetic device-related infections usually involves removal of the device.

items.question: Which toxin is responsible for more than 90% of menstrual TSS cases?
items.explanation: More than 90% of menstrual TSS cases are caused by TSST-1.
items.reference: Staphylococcal Infections · CLINICAL SYNDROMES (Table 152-1) (part 4) | Clinical Criteria

items.question: What is the minimum systolic blood pressure for adults to meet the hypotension criterion in TSS?
items.explanation: Hypotension is defined as a systolic blood pressure ≤90 mmHg for adults.
items.reference: Staphylococcal Infections · CLINICAL SYNDROMES (Table 152-1) (part 4) | Clinical Criteria

items.question: Which of the following symptoms reflects renal involvement in TSS?
items.explanation: Renal involvement is indicated by pyuria (≥5 leukocytes per high-power field) in the absence of a urinary tract infection.
items.reference: Staphylococcal Infections · CLINICAL SYNDROMES (Table 152-1) (part 4) | Clinical Criteria

items.subtopic: Laboratory Criteria | Case Classification | NON-S. AUREUS STAPHYLOCOCCAL INFECTIONS
items.question: Which of the following tests must yield negative results to meet the laboratory criteria for a probable case classification?
options: Blood or cerebrospinal fluid cultures for another pathogen
Serologic tests for Rocky Mountain spotted fever, leptospirosis, or measles
Both blood or cerebrospinal fluid cultures and serologic tests as mentioned above
items.explanation: Negative results in both blood or CSF cultures for another pathogen and serologic tests for specific diseases are required.
items.reference: Staphylococcal Infections · Laboratory Criteria | Case Classification | NON-S. AUREUS STAPHYLOCOCCAL INFECTIONS

items.subtopic: Laboratory Criteria | Case Classification | NON-S. AUREUS STAPHYLOCOCCAL INFECTIONS
items.question: What is necessary to classify a case as confirmed?
options: Meeting laboratory criteria and fulfilling four of the five clinical criteria
Meeting laboratory criteria and fulfilling all five clinical criteria, including desquamation
Positive blood cultures for S. aureus
items.explanation: A confirmed case meets the laboratory criteria and fulfills all five clinical criteria.
items.reference: Staphylococcal Infections · Laboratory Criteria | Case Classification | NON-S. AUREUS STAPHYLOCOCCAL INFECTIONS

items.subtopic: Laboratory Criteria | Case Classification | NON-S. AUREUS STAPHYLOCOCCAL INFECTIONS
items.question: Which of the following is a common cause of prosthetic-device infections?
items.explanation: Non-S. aureus staphylococci (NSaS) are common causes of prosthetic-device infections.
items.reference: Staphylococcal Infections · Laboratory Criteria | Case Classification | NON-S. AUREUS STAPHYLOCOCCAL INFECTIONS

items.question: Which process is essential for S. epidermidis to form a protective biofilm on prosthetic devices?
options: Elaboration of extracellular polysaccharide (glycocalyx or slime)
Production of clumping factor and lipase
Adherence to uroepithelial cells via hemagglutinin/adhesin
items.explanation: S. epidermidis elaborates extracellular polysaccharide (glycocalyx or slime) to form a protective biofilm.

items.question: What is the role of host matrix molecules like fibrinogen in prosthetic-device infections?
options: They serve as bridging ligands for initial bacterial attachment.
They are responsible for biofilm formation.
They prevent systemic signs of sepsis.
items.explanation: Host matrix molecules such as fibrinogen facilitate initial bacterial attachment to the device surface.

items.question: Which clinical finding is suggestive of true bacteremia in NSaS infections?
options: Presence of adhesins on uroepithelial cells.
Isolation of different strains from separate cultures.
Growth of the strain within 48 hours.
Absence of systemic signs of sepsis.
items.explanation: Growth of the strain within 48 hours is suggestive of true bacteremia in NSaS infections.

items.subtopic: CLINICAL SYNDROMES | GENERAL PRINCIPLES OF THERAPY | DURATION OF ANTIMICROBIAL THERAPY
items.question: Which pathogen is noted for causing greater mortality and rapid valvular destruction with abscess formation in preterm infants and neutropenic patients?
items.explanation: S. lugdunensis is noted for causing greater mortality and rapid valvular destruction with abscess formation in preterm infants and neutropenic patients.
items.reference: Staphylococcal Infections · CLINICAL SYNDROMES | GENERAL PRINCIPLES OF THERAPY | DURATION OF ANTIMICROBIAL THERAPY

items.subtopic: CLINICAL SYNDROMES | GENERAL PRINCIPLES OF THERAPY | DURATION OF ANTIMICROBIAL THERAPY
items.question: What is generally necessary to rule out endocarditis in the management of staphylococcal infections?
items.explanation: Transesophageal echocardiography is generally necessary to rule out endocarditis because clinical or laboratory findings cannot reliably detect cardiac involvement.
items.reference: Staphylococcal Infections · CLINICAL SYNDROMES | GENERAL PRINCIPLES OF THERAPY | DURATION OF ANTIMICROBIAL THERAPY

items.subtopic: CLINICAL SYNDROMES | GENERAL PRINCIPLES OF THERAPY | DURATION OF ANTIMICROBIAL THERAPY
items.question: What is the recommended duration of therapy for S. aureus bacteremia with a high risk of complications?
items.explanation: Therapy for S. aureus bacteremia is generally prolonged (4–6 weeks) due to the high risk of complications like endocarditis and metastatic foci of infection.
items.reference: Staphylococcal Infections · CLINICAL SYNDROMES | GENERAL PRINCIPLES OF THERAPY | DURATION OF ANTIMICROBIAL THERAPY

items.question: Which antibiotic is no longer used as a semisynthetic penicillinase-resistant penicillin for treating staphylococcal infections?
items.explanation: Methicillin, the first of the SPRPs, is no longer used.
items.reference: Staphylococcal Infections · CHOICE OF ANTIMICROBIAL AGENTS (part 1)

items.question: Which antibiotic should be avoided for treating staphylococcal infections due to considerably less activity?
items.explanation: Some third-generation cephalosporins, such as ceftazidime, have considerably less activity and should be avoided.
items.reference: Staphylococcal Infections · CHOICE OF ANTIMICROBIAL AGENTS (part 1)

items.question: What is the primary mechanism responsible for methicillin resistance in staphylococci?
items.explanation: Methicillin resistance is due to the production of a novel penicillin-binding protein (PBP2a) synthesized by the mecA gene.
items.reference: Staphylococcal Infections · CHOICE OF ANTIMICROBIAL AGENTS (part 1)

items.question: Which mechanism is primarily responsible for vancomycin-intermediate S. aureus (VISA) resistance?
options: Horizontal gene transfer from Enterococcus faecalis
Mutations in topoisomerase IV or DNA gyrase genes
An abnormally thick cell wall trapping vancomycin
items.explanation: VISA resistance is partly due to an abnormally thick cell wall that traps vancomycin.
items.reference: Staphylococcal Infections · CHOICE OF ANTIMICROBIAL AGENTS (part 2)

items.question: What gene is responsible for the synthesis of D-Ala-D-Lac in VRSA, leading to resistance?
items.explanation: The vanA gene is responsible for the synthesis of D-Ala-D-Lac in VRSA.
items.reference: Staphylococcal Infections · CHOICE OF ANTIMICROBIAL AGENTS (part 2)

items.question: Which antimicrobial agent is not effective in respiratory infections due to its mechanism of action?
items.explanation: Daptomycin disrupts the cytoplasmic membrane and is not effective in respiratory infections.
items.reference: Staphylococcal Infections · CHOICE OF ANTIMICROBIAL AGENTS (part 2)

items.question: Which drug has been associated with serious adverse reactions such as thrombocytopenia and lactic acidosis?
items.explanation: Linezolid can cause serious adverse reactions including thrombocytopenia and lactic acidosis.
items.reference: Staphylococcal Infections · CHOICE OF ANTIMICROBIAL AGENTS (part 2)

items.question: Which antibiotic is approved for use in SSTIs and intraabdominal infections caused by S. aureus but not recommended for invasive infections?
items.explanation: Tigecycline is approved for SSTIs and intraabdominal infections but not recommended for invasive infections.
items.reference: Staphylococcal Infections · CHOICE OF ANTIMICROBIAL AGENTS (part 3)

items.question: Which antibiotic has excellent activity against both MRSA and MSSA, and is effective in treating complicated staphylococcal bacteremias, SSTIs, and pneumonia?
items.explanation: Ceftobiprole has excellent activity against both MRSA and MSSA.
items.reference: Staphylococcal Infections · CHOICE OF ANTIMICROBIAL AGENTS (part 3)

items.question: Which of the following antibiotics is NOT routinely recommended for invasive infections due to concerns about nephrotoxicity?
items.explanation: Gentamicin is not routinely recommended for invasive infections due to nephrotoxicity concerns.
items.reference: Staphylococcal Infections · CHOICE OF ANTIMICROBIAL AGENTS (part 3)

items.question: Which combination is recommended for treating persistent MRSA bacteremia when isolates display reduced susceptibility to antimicrobial agents?
options: Daptomycin or vancomycin with a β-lactam antibiotic (e.g., ceftaroline)
items.explanation: The combination of daptomycin or vancomycin with a β-lactam antibiotic (e.g., ceftaroline) enhances bactericidal activity.
items.reference: Staphylococcal Infections · ANTIMICROBIAL THERAPY FOR SELECTED SETTINGS

items.question: For native-valve endocarditis caused by MRSA, which of the following is recommended?
options: Vancomycin or daptomycin with a β-lactam antibiotic
Vancomycin (15–20 mg/kg every 8–12 h) or daptomycin (6–10 mg/kg every 24 h)
items.explanation: Vancomycin or daptomycin is recommended, with specific dosing guidelines provided.
items.reference: Staphylococcal Infections · ANTIMICROBIAL THERAPY FOR SELECTED SETTINGS

items.question: What is the mainstay of therapy for treating toxic shock syndrome (TSS)?
options: Antibiotic combination with clindamycin and a semisynthetic penicillin or vancomycin
Supportive treatment including fluids and pressors
items.explanation: The mainstay of therapy for TSS is the treatment of shock, primarily with fluids and pressors.
items.reference: Staphylococcal Infections · ANTIMICROBIAL THERAPY FOR SELECTED SETTINGS

items.question: For prosthetic-valve endocarditis, what additional intervention is often necessary besides antibiotic therapy?
options: Repeated aspiration or arthroscopy of the affected joint
items.explanation: Surgery is often necessary in addition to antibiotic therapy for prosthetic-valve endocarditis.
items.reference: Staphylococcal Infections · ANTIMICROBIAL THERAPY FOR SELECTED SETTINGS

items.subtopic: NONTRADITIONAL APPROACHES TO ANTISTAPHYLOCOCCAL THERAPY | ☐ PREVENTION
items.question: Which of the following strategies has been used to reduce the incidence of postsurgical infections among people nasally colonized with S. aureus?
options: Application of capsular polysaccharide-protein conjugate vaccine
Prophylactic use of clumping factor antibody
items.explanation: Decolonization with mupirocin and chlorhexidine has been shown to reduce postsurgical infections in those colonized with S. aureus.
items.reference: Staphylococcal Infections · NONTRADITIONAL APPROACHES TO ANTISTAPHYLOCOCCAL THERAPY | ☐ PREVENTION

items.subtopic: NONTRADITIONAL APPROACHES TO ANTISTAPHYLOCOCCAL THERAPY | ☐ PREVENTION
items.question: Which of the following is a primary prevention strategy for S. aureus infections in hospitals?
options: Use of antivirulence strategies targeting virulence determinants
Hand washing and appropriate isolation procedures
items.explanation: Primary prevention involves hand washing and careful attention to appropriate isolation procedures.
items.reference: Staphylococcal Infections · NONTRADITIONAL APPROACHES TO ANTISTAPHYLOCOCCAL THERAPY | ☐ PREVENTION

items.subtopic: NONTRADITIONAL APPROACHES TO ANTISTAPHYLOCOCCAL THERAPY | ☐ PREVENTION
items.question: What has been the outcome of immunization strategies for preventing S. aureus infections in clinical trials?
options: Successful in both prophylaxis and therapy
Successful only as a therapeutic intervention
Not successful in either prophylaxis or therapy
items.explanation: Immunization strategies have not been successful for either prophylaxis or therapy in clinical trials.
items.reference: Staphylococcal Infections · NONTRADITIONAL APPROACHES TO ANTISTAPHYLOCOCCAL THERAPY | ☐ PREVENTION

items.statement: Coagulase-negative staphylococci are generally more virulent than S. aureus.
items.explanation: Coagulase-negative staphylococci are less virulent than S. aureus.
items.reference: Staphylococcal Infections · Staphylococcal Infections | MICROBIOLOGY AND TAXONOMY

items.statement: S. aureus is capable of fermenting mannitol.
items.explanation: S. aureus ferments mannitol.
items.reference: Staphylococcal Infections · Staphylococcal Infections | MICROBIOLOGY AND TAXONOMY

items.statement: MRSA is no longer a concern in healthcare settings.
items.explanation: CA-MRSA strains have been responsible for an increasing number of nosocomial infections.

items.statement: Most individuals with S. aureus infections are infected by a strain not part of their own flora.
items.explanation: Most individuals who develop S. aureus infections become infected with a strain that is already a part of their own commensal flora.

items.statement: Staphylococcal toxic shock syndrome requires infection for toxin elaboration.
items.explanation: Toxin-mediated diseases like TSS can occur without infection, as conditions allowing toxin elaboration suffice.

items.statement: Methicillin resistance in S. aureus is associated with the presence of SCCmec.
items.explanation: The mecA gene responsible for methicillin resistance is found within staphylococcal cassette chromosome mec (SCCmec).

items.statement: S. aureus strains with capsular types 5 and 8 are most commonly associated with human infections.
items.explanation: Most human S. aureus infections are due to strains with capsular types 5 and 8.

items.statement: Protein A prevents opsonophagocytosis by binding the Fc portion of IgG subclasses.
items.explanation: Protein A acts as an Fc receptor, preventing opsonophagocytosis by PMNs.

items.statement: Antitoxin antibodies are protective against illness in TSS, staphylococcal food poisoning, and SSSS.
items.explanation: Both epidemiologic data and animal studies suggest antitoxin antibodies protect against these illnesses.

items.statement: Enterotoxins are heat labile and cannot survive conditions that kill the bacteria.
items.explanation: Enterotoxins are heat stable and can survive conditions that kill the bacteria.

items.statement: S. aureus is rarely a contaminant in blood cultures.
items.explanation: S. aureus is rarely a blood culture contaminant.

items.statement: Routine cultures of infected material are usually negative for staphylococcal infections.
items.explanation: Routine cultures of infected material usually are positive for staphylococcal infections.

items.statement: S. aureus is a common cause of bone infections in children, often involving the long bones.
items.explanation: Hematogenous osteomyelitis in children most often involves the long bones and is commonly caused by S. aureus.

items.statement: Routine x-rays are always sufficient for diagnosing osteomyelitis.
items.explanation: Findings on routine x-rays may be normal for up to 14 days after the onset of symptoms, making them insufficient alone for diagnosis.

items.statement: S. aureus is a leading cause of community-acquired pneumonia.
items.explanation: Community-acquired respiratory tract infections due to S. aureus often follow viral infections, not primarily causing them.

items.statement: The incidence of complications from S. aureus bacteremia increases with the duration of the bacteremia.
items.explanation: The incidence of complications such as sepsis and endocarditis increases with the duration of bacteremia.

items.statement: S. aureus is rarely associated with urinary tract infections.
items.explanation: Urinary tract infections are infrequently caused by S. aureus.

items.statement: S. aureus food poisoning is characterized by a slow onset of symptoms.
items.explanation: The onset of illness in staphylococcal food poisoning is rapid, occurring within 1–6 hours.

items.statement: Clinical S. aureus infection is necessary for TSS to occur.
items.explanation: Evidence of clinical S. aureus infection is not a prerequisite for TSS.
items.reference: Staphylococcal Infections · CLINICAL SYNDROMES (Table 152-1) (part 4) | Clinical Criteria

items.statement: Desquamation in TSS occurs during the acute phase of illness.
items.explanation: Desquamation occurs during convalescence, usually 1–2 weeks after onset.
items.reference: Staphylococcal Infections · CLINICAL SYNDROMES (Table 152-1) (part 4) | Clinical Criteria

items.subtopic: Laboratory Criteria | Case Classification | NON-S. AUREUS STAPHYLOCOCCAL INFECTIONS
items.statement: Diagnosis of TSS depends on a single specific finding.
items.explanation: TSS diagnosis relies on a constellation of findings, not one specific finding.
items.reference: Staphylococcal Infections · Laboratory Criteria | Case Classification | NON-S. AUREUS STAPHYLOCOCCAL INFECTIONS

items.subtopic: Laboratory Criteria | Case Classification | NON-S. AUREUS STAPHYLOCOCCAL INFECTIONS
items.statement: Staphylococcus epidermidis is part of the normal human flora.
items.explanation: S. epidermidis is a common component of the skin's normal bacterial population.
items.reference: Staphylococcal Infections · Laboratory Criteria | Case Classification | NON-S. AUREUS STAPHYLOCOCCAL INFECTIONS

items.statement: Staphylococcus lugdunensis and Staphylococcus schleiferi are associated with less serious infections compared to other NSaS.
items.explanation: These species produce more serious infections like native-valve endocarditis and osteomyelitis.

items.statement: The ica genes are commonly found in strains of S. epidermidis associated with mucosal surface colonization.
items.explanation: Ica genes are more common in device-associated infections, not mucosal surface colonization.

items.subtopic: CLINICAL SYNDROMES | GENERAL PRINCIPLES OF THERAPY | DURATION OF ANTIMICROBIAL THERAPY
items.statement: Parenteral therapy is always necessary to complete a course of treatment for invasive staphylococcal infections such as endocarditis or osteomyelitis.
items.explanation: Recent studies have shown that parenteral therapy is not always necessary for completing the treatment in carefully selected patients with uncomplicated staphylococcal bacteremia.
items.reference: Staphylococcal Infections · CLINICAL SYNDROMES | GENERAL PRINCIPLES OF THERAPY | DURATION OF ANTIMICROBIAL THERAPY

items.subtopic: CLINICAL SYNDROMES | GENERAL PRINCIPLES OF THERAPY | DURATION OF ANTIMICROBIAL THERAPY
items.statement: NSaS account for approximately 5% of native-valve endocarditis cases.
items.explanation: NSaS cause about 5% of native-valve endocarditis cases as noted in the passage.
items.reference: Staphylococcal Infections · CLINICAL SYNDROMES | GENERAL PRINCIPLES OF THERAPY | DURATION OF ANTIMICROBIAL THERAPY

items.statement: All cephalosporins are effective against MRSA.
items.explanation: Resistance to methicillin indicates resistance to all SPRPs as well as to all cephalosporins, except ceftaroline.
items.reference: Staphylococcal Infections · CHOICE OF ANTIMICROBIAL AGENTS (part 1)

items.statement: Vancomycin is recommended for the treatment of invasive MRSA infections.
items.explanation: Either vancomycin or daptomycin is recommended as the drug of choice for treating invasive MRSA infections.
items.reference: Staphylococcal Infections · CHOICE OF ANTIMICROBIAL AGENTS (part 1)

items.statement: Quinolones are recommended for the treatment of MRSA infections.
items.explanation: Quinolones are not recommended due to increased resistance, especially in MRSA.
items.reference: Staphylococcal Infections · CHOICE OF ANTIMICROBIAL AGENTS (part 2)

items.statement: Ceftaroline is effective against MRSA strains with reduced susceptibility to vancomycin and daptomycin.
items.explanation: Ceftaroline has bactericidal activity against MRSA, including those resistant to vancomycin and daptomycin.
items.reference: Staphylococcal Infections · CHOICE OF ANTIMICROBIAL AGENTS (part 2)

items.statement: Rifampin is used in the treatment of prosthetic device-related infections.
items.explanation: Rifampin continues to be used for prosthetic device-related infections.
items.reference: Staphylococcal Infections · CHOICE OF ANTIMICROBIAL AGENTS (part 3)

items.statement: Clinical studies have documented a therapeutic benefit from the addition of gentamicin to single-drug regimens.
items.explanation: Studies have not documented a therapeutic benefit from adding gentamicin.
items.reference: Staphylococcal Infections · CHOICE OF ANTIMICROBIAL AGENTS (part 3)

items.statement: Daptomycin is always preferable over vancomycin when elevated vancomycin MICs are common.
items.explanation: It remains uncertain whether daptomycin is preferable when elevated vancomycin MICs are common.
items.reference: Staphylococcal Infections · ANTIMICROBIAL THERAPY FOR SELECTED SETTINGS

items.statement: Intravenous immunoglobulin is recommended for the treatment of toxic shock syndrome.
items.explanation: The benefit of intravenous immunoglobulin in treating TSS is uncertain.
items.reference: Staphylococcal Infections · ANTIMICROBIAL THERAPY FOR SELECTED SETTINGS

items.subtopic: NONTRADITIONAL APPROACHES TO ANTISTAPHYLOCOCCAL THERAPY | ☐ PREVENTION
items.statement: Decolonization strategies have been unsuccessful in reducing the incidence of postsurgical infections.
items.explanation: Decolonization strategies, such as using mupirocin and chlorhexidine, have successfully reduced the incidence of postsurgical infections.
items.reference: Staphylococcal Infections · NONTRADITIONAL APPROACHES TO ANTISTAPHYLOCOCCAL THERAPY | ☐ PREVENTION

items.subtopic: NONTRADITIONAL APPROACHES TO ANTISTAPHYLOCOCCAL THERAPY | ☐ PREVENTION
items.statement: Scandinavian countries have been successful in preventing MRSA introduction and dissemination through careful screening and isolation.
items.explanation: Careful screening for MRSA carriage and strict isolation practices have helped prevent MRSA introduction and dissemination in Scandinavian hospitals.
items.reference: Staphylococcal Infections · NONTRADITIONAL APPROACHES TO ANTISTAPHYLOCOCCAL THERAPY | ☐ PREVENTION

items.assertion: S. aureus is responsible for a wide range of infections, from minor to life-threatening.
items.reason: S. aureus causes disease through both toxin- and non-toxin-mediated mechanisms.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Staphylococcal Infections · Staphylococcal Infections | MICROBIOLOGY AND TAXONOMY

items.assertion: S. aureus colonization rates are elevated among individuals with damaged skin.
items.reason: Damaged skin serves as a potential reservoir for S. aureus infections.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.

items.assertion: MRSA has become less prevalent in community settings over recent decades.
items.reason: Outbreaks of CA-MRSA infections have been reported among diverse groups worldwide.
items.explanation: The assertion is false, while the reason is true; MRSA prevalence in community settings has increased.

items.assertion: Successful invasion by S. aureus requires the sequential expression of bacterial elements.
items.reason: Bacterial adhesins are needed to initiate colonization, followed by enzyme release for nutritional support and tissue spread.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true; the reason correctly explains why sequential expression is necessary.

items.assertion: S. aureus colonization of body surfaces is independent of host factors.
items.reason: Colonization involves attachment to keratinized epithelial cells and other nasal flora influences.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; colonization is influenced by host factors, among other elements.

items.assertion: S. aureus can survive within phagocytes.
items.reason: Internalization provides a sanctuary against host defenses, especially in hepatic Kupffer cells during bacteremias.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true; internalization by phagocytes protects S. aureus from host defenses.

items.assertion: Panton-Valentine leukocidin is associated with necrotizing postinfluenza pneumonia.
items.reason: The gene for Panton-Valentine leukocidin is linked to SSTIs and necrotizing postinfluenza pneumonia.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, with the reason correctly explaining the association.

items.assertion: The exfoliative toxins cause SSSS by cleaving desmosomal cadherins in the superficial layer of the skin.
items.reason: This action triggers a split in the epidermis at the granular level, leading to superficial desquamation.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.

items.assertion: PCR-based assays have become a common tool in the rapid diagnosis of S. aureus infection.
items.reason: Routine cultures are often positive, but PCR provides faster results.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason is not the correct explanation for the assertion.

items.assertion: S. aureus is the most common cause of septic arthritis in native joints.
items.reason: Septic arthritis presents with intense pain, swelling, and fever in the affected joint.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why S. aureus is a common cause of septic arthritis.

items.assertion: Pyomyositis is an infection seen primarily in tropical climates and immunocompromised patients.
items.reason: It arises from occult bacteremia and presents with fever, swelling, and pain overlying the involved muscle.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why pyomyositis occurs in these populations.

items.assertion: S. aureus endocarditis is most commonly seen in patients with a history of injection drug use.
items.reason: Acute right-sided tricuspid valvular S. aureus endocarditis often occurs in patients who inject drugs.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.

items.assertion: Blood cultures are usually positive in patients with S. aureus endocarditis.
items.reason: In the absence of antecedent antibiotic therapy, blood cultures are almost uniformly positive.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.

items.assertion: S. aureus infections associated with community-acquired MRSA are often life-threatening.
items.reason: The increased virulence of CA-MRSA strains leads to severe infections like necrotizing fasciitis and sepsis.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.

items.assertion: The diagnosis of staphylococcal food poisoning is established by demonstrating bacteria in the patient.
items.reason: Staphylococcal food poisoning results from toxin-producing S. aureus, and symptoms resolve rapidly.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because diagnosis involves detecting enterotoxin in food, not necessarily bacteria in the patient.

items.assertion: TSS is characterized by fever and hypotension.
items.reason: Fever ≥102.0°F and systolic blood pressure ≤90 mmHg are part of the clinical criteria for TSS.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Staphylococcal Infections · CLINICAL SYNDROMES (Table 152-1) (part 4) | Clinical Criteria

items.subtopic: CLINICAL SYNDROMES | GENERAL PRINCIPLES OF THERAPY | DURATION OF ANTIMICROBIAL THERAPY
items.assertion: Therapy for S. aureus bacteremia is generally prolonged.
items.reason: The high risk of complications such as endocarditis and metastatic foci of infection necessitates a longer duration of therapy.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why therapy for S. aureus bacteremia is generally prolonged.
items.reference: Staphylococcal Infections · CLINICAL SYNDROMES | GENERAL PRINCIPLES OF THERAPY | DURATION OF ANTIMICROBIAL THERAPY

items.assertion: Telavancin should be avoided in patients with renal disease.
items.reason: Telavancin has potential nephrotoxicity.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Staphylococcal Infections · CHOICE OF ANTIMICROBIAL AGENTS (part 2)

items.assertion: Daptomycin resistance can emerge during therapy.
items.reason: Patients previously treated with vancomycin may have elevated daptomycin MICs.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason is not a direct explanation for the emergence of resistance.
items.reference: Staphylococcal Infections · CHOICE OF ANTIMICROBIAL AGENTS (part 2)

items.assertion: Combining daptomycin with a β-lactam antibiotic enhances its bactericidal activity.
items.reason: The combination reduces the bacterial cell-surface charge, allowing enhanced daptomycin binding.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Staphylococcal Infections · ANTIMICROBIAL THERAPY FOR SELECTED SETTINGS

items.assertion: Glucocorticoids are recommended for treating toxic shock syndrome.
items.reason: They help in reducing inflammation associated with TSS.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; glucocorticoids are not recommended for the treatment of TSS.
items.reference: Staphylococcal Infections · ANTIMICROBIAL THERAPY FOR SELECTED SETTINGS
